text: This is a seventy seven year old patient who has been diagnosed with achalasia. The video will illustrate a robotic helleromyotomy and dwarf endoplication.
The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to take down the gastropag limit, exposing the right crus. The esophagus is dissected out, and the attachments of the stomach to the left crus are separated.
The surgeon performs mediastinal dissection of the esophagus using a harmonic scalpel and blunt dissection techniques, carefully separating the esophagus from surrounding mediastinal structures.
The surgeon dissects and encircles the vagus nerve, fully mobilizing it away from the esophagus to prepare for the myotomy.

The surgeon initiates the myotomy using electrocautery to separate the longitudinal muscles of the esophagus, revealing some circular muscle layers. These circular layers are cauterized to expose the underlying submucosa.

The robotic system is used to grasp both sides of the myotomy, facilitating the delivery of the esophagus into the abdomen. A hook is employed to complete the myotomy, with care taken to avoid thermal injury to the submucosa by occasionally cooling the hook on adjacent tissues.
The surgeon uses retraction arms to pull the esophagus down into the abdomen, folding the muscle layers outward like an open book to facilitate further dissection.

The surgeon extends the myotomy up to the level of the inferior pulmonary veins and down onto the gastroesophageal junction, continuing approximately two to three centimeters onto the stomach. The vagus nerve is visible traversing the myotomy site.

The camera provides a view of the completed myotomy extending from the mid esophagus down onto the stomach, spanning over six centimeters above the gastroesophageal junction and two centimeters below it. The short gastric vessels are divided to facilitate mobilization of the stomach fundus for the Dor fundoplication.
The surgeon begins the repair by suturing the left crus of the diaphragm to the left side of the myotomy muscle using a 2-0 Ethibond suture.

The surgeon tacks the angle of His to the diaphragmatic crus. The fundus of the stomach is then positioned over the esophagus, covering the myotomy. A stitch is placed into the stomach, through the right side of the myotomy muscle, and secured to the right crus.

The surgeon places a stitch from the fundus of the stomach to the right side of the myotomy, followed by suturing to the right cruce. The stomach is then tacked to the anterior defect, effectively closing the defect and the hiatus.
This patient did well postoperatively. Post-op esophagram demonstrated no leak. The patient was sent home on a full liquid diet on postoperative day number one.
